data.title: Tonix Pharmaceuticals: August PDUFA Date For Fibromyalgia Med A Risky Bet
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Tonix Pharmaceuticals Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:TNXP) for Seeking Alpha back in March 2025, issuing a "Hold" recommendation, with stock trading at $30 per share. Shares have, in fact, gained >25% since that call.

The call was based on several factors. I noted that Tonix markets and sells two commercially available drugs to treat acute migraine, Zembrace and Tosymra, but these two drugs make negligible sales - for example, in Q1 2025, their combined revenues totaled $2.4m, down from $2.5m in the prior year.

I also noted the company's overwhelming priority was to secure approval for its lead pipeline candidate, TNX-102 SL, or Cyclobenzaprine HCl Sublingual Tablets, to treat patients with fibromyalgia.

According to the Mayo Clinic:

Fibromyalgia is a long-term condition that involves widespread body pain. The pain happens along with fatigue. It also can involve issues with sleep, memory and mood. Researchers think that fibromyalgia affects the way the brain and spinal cord process painful and nonpainful signals. That increases your overall sensitivity to pain.

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention ("CDC"), fibromyalgia affects ~4m US adults.

Tonix has submitted its New Drug Application ("NDA") to the FDA based on - according to its Q1 2025 10Q submission - "two statistically significant Phase 3 studies for the management of fibromyalgia," and has been assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act (“PDUFA”) goal date of August 15, 2025 i.e. that is the date by which the FDA must rule whether to approve or reject the drug.

When I covered Tonix in March, shares had leaped >200% in value as management shared news that the FDA would not convene an advisory committee ("AdCom"), to discuss and potentially vote on whether TNX-102 SL should be approved for commercial use.

While an AdCom's decision is not binding, the FDA usually - but not always - follows its advice, but when the agency opts against convening an AdCom, it is often interpreted by the market as a sign it has made up its mind to approve the drug.

Finally, however, I noted that:

Tonix completed a 1-for-32 reverse stock split in May 2022, then a 1-for-6.25 reverse stock split in May 2023, a 1-32 split in June last year, and remarkably, a 1-100 split in February this year. Regarding the last split, Tonix stated that it was:

That means that, on a 1-year basis, Tonix stock is down >90%, and its market cap valuation is presently $285m, making it a higher risk investment opportunity.

Tonix has been working on TNX-102 for more than a decade, and prior versions of the drug failed to meet endpoints in key clinical studies (discussed in more detail in my previous post), which explains why the share price has suffered, and why there may be skepticism around whether Tonix can secure approval for TNX-102, and even if it did, whether it could turn its drug into a commercial success.

In April, Tonix stock fell as low as $15 per share, but just a few days ago, had climbed >$42 per share, before dropping ~8% in trading so far today. In this update, I'll update on any developments since my last coverage, try to explain recent share price volatility, and speculate about where I see the share price heading next.

Tonix - Re-Visiting TNX-102 Approval Shot

No new fibromyalgia drugs have been approved in over 15 years - currently approved meds include Eli Lilly's (LLY) Cymbalta, and its generics, Viatris' (VTRS) Lyrica, and its generics, Savella (milnacipran), marketed by AbbVie (ABBV), and cyclobezaprine, the underlying ingredient in TNX-102, sold as Flexeril since 1977 for treatment of muscle spasms, and its generics.

As we can see above (slide taken from a recent Tonix presentation) Tonix has done its market research, which suggests physicians are broadly dissatisfied with current treatment options.

Tonix's solution - a sublingual formulation of cyclobenzaprine ("CBP") seems to make sense. The underlying ingredient is a non-opioid, the sublingual formulation administration induces rapid drug exposure, and a durable (14 week) reduction in pain has been shown in two separate pivotal studies.

While Tonix initially failed with a 2.8mg version, its latest version uses a 5.6mg dose. The FDA gave the drug fast Track Designation in July 2024, and in its investor presentation Tonix provides a lot of detailed information around CBP and its positive treatment effects.

The recent uptrend in the value of shares may suggest the market believes the FDA will approve the drug, however we may note the small share float of 7.32m, plus the fact that ~12% of the share float is apparently held short.

As of Q1, Tonix reported $131.7m of cash, although the company states in its earnings press release that this "will meet its planned operating and capital expenditure requirements into the second quarter of 2026," so investors should probably expect more dilutive fundraising activities. Doubtless, Tonix will hope to raise at a much higher share price in August on the back of an FDA approval. Net loss in Q1 was $(16.83m), or $2.84 per share.

Frankly, I am 50/50 on whether TNX-102 will be approved, but interestingly, Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST) is set to find out any day now whether its sublingual drug candidate Anaphylm™ (epinephrine) will gain approval to treat severe allergic reactions, which could be a useful yardstick to assess the FDA's stance on sublingual medications.

I am personally not completely convinced they are a significant enough improvement on what is already available to warrant approval, and as mentioned, even if TNX-102 is approved, there is limited funding to support a launch campaign, and we may also note multiple drugs are prescribed off-label for fibromyalgia, so persuading patients to switch away from their favoured treatment may be problematic.

Tonix - Opportunities Beyond Lead Candidate

As we can see above, Tonix has initiated a Phase 2 study of TNX-102 SL in acute stress disorder - the dosing of a first patient in this study was announced earlier this month, and may have played a significant role in the recent share price gains.

There is also an anti-CD40 monoclonal antibody in development for organ transplant rejection / autoimmune conditions, and a horsepox vaccine. Make no mistake, TNX-102 is the key value driver here, but at times when outbreaks of Mpox are reported - which has happened on a couple of occasions over the past couple of years - share prices of companies developing antidote's do tend to spike.

While emphasizing the primary focus on TNX-102 in the Q1 earnings release - Tonix CEO Dr Seth Lederman stated that "we are building out our commercial team for the anticipated product launch in the fourth quarter of this year" - the CEO did also state that he was "encouraged" by positive Phase 1 results for TNX-1500, and hoped to share data for this asset and the mpox / smallpox vaccine later this year.

Concluding Thoughts - Volatility Looks Guaranteed - A Speculative Buy?

As mentioned above, while recent share price gains imply positive market sentiment, small purchases of stock can likely create significant upside or downside movements, so it is best not to pay much attention to recent swings.

The August PDUFA data for TNX-102 can only be described as a critical moment for Tonix - succeed, and I suspect shares will spike on news of a first new fibromylagia treatment in 15 years. Fail, and after four reverse stock splits in two years, I suspect Tonix shares would plunge in value, putting the company's future as a public entity in doubt.

Ultimately, since I cannot much in the way of persuasive evidence to suggest TNX-102 will not be approved by the FDA in August, I favor the bull case for share, although only in the short term. I would be less convinced that Tonix can convert its innovative fibromyalgia solution into a profitable, triple-digit million selling product.

I could be wrong of course, and as mentioned previously, it's important to understand that this is a <$300m market cap company with limited finances and therefore represents a very high risk investment opportunity.

I would certainly wait to see what happens with Aquestive before making any moves, but if that company's sublingual product is approved, I'd make Tonix a marginally more "double your money" than "lose your shirt" opportunity, although only by around 55:45, and I'd be looking to sell shares quickly post-approval.

data.title: Major May Rally, Stocks Soar Amid AI Optimism And Tariff Relief
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) ended just shy of posting its best May since 1990. Stocks tallied strong gains, despite ongoing uncertainties surrounding the trade war. Indeed, traders appear to be largely looking past changing tariff headlines, instead focusing on what turned out to be a stellar Q1 earnings season and consumers who continue to spend. Oh, an AI is back in vogue, too.

The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) tacked on 6%, while the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) rose a strong 9%. U.S. small caps underperformed modestly, but the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) returned 5%.

International stocks built on the big 2025 advance. The Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA) jumped more than 5%, while the Vanguard Emerging Markets ETF (VWO) added 4%.

In the bond market, the iShares Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) was down 1% amid Treasury rates that backed up through much of May. Yields did stabilize over the back half of the month, however. On a year-over-year basis, AGG has returned a respectable 5.5%, including dividends.

Elsewhere, bitcoin (BTC-USD, IBIT) was up 10% in May but finished the month well off its new all-time high. Gold (GLD) was flat, along with sideways action in the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY). An early-month bump helped oil score a 4% rally.

Stocks Charged Higher in May, U.S. Large Caps Led

Digging into the sectors, it was all about tech and AI. The best of the 11 S&P 500 areas was Information Technology (XLK). The largest sector rose 9%. Runner-up was Industrials (XLI), driven higher by enduring strength from the Aerospace & Defense industry and cyclical stocks exposed to AI—perhaps we should add GE Aerospace (GE) and GE Vernova (GEV) to the list of so-called Magnificent stocks.

Bringing up the rear—by a wide margin—was Health Care (XLV). UnitedHealth Group (UNH) plunged amid regulatory concerns, while Eli Lilly (LLY) drifted toward the bottom of its now year-long trading range.

Broadly, 20%-plus rallies from Tesla (TSLA) and Nvidia (NVDA) helped U.S. large caps to a strong May showing. Only Apple (AAPL) was down among the Mag 7.

Tech Led in May, Health Care Was the Lone Negative Sector

Driving price action has been resilient macro data. The April jobs report was yet another in a string of generally "good, not great" views of the labor market. Though the unemployment rate ticked up marginally, the headline establishment survey employment gain was close to 200,000 for a second consecutive month.

Average hourly earnings ticked down to 3.8%, helping to assuage inflation concerns. Ahead of the May NFP report, due out next Friday, expectations call for a payroll gain of about 140,000 with an unchanged jobless rate.

U.S. Labor Remains Good, Not Great

The consumer remains in focus, given the precarious situation around tariffs. As Liberation Day came and went, it was clear that households and businesses were stockpiling to avoid potentially much higher price tags on goods that were supposed to arrive in May. So far, high-frequency consumption data point to ongoing spending strength. We’ll know more when the May Retail Sales report is released in mid-June.

For now, Johnson Redbook Retail Sales data, OpenTable reservation bookings, and record-high TSA checkpoint numbers point to solid spending activity.

U.S. Retail Sales Strong at +5.2% YoY

On the inflation front, there was good news on May’s final trading day. PCE Price Index figures for April revealed a continued trend of disinflation. The year-on-year headline PCE Price Index change ticked down to 2.1%, the lowest in four years, and within a whisker of the Fed’s 2% target.

Of course, expectations are for a bump up in both PCE inflation and the CPI rate as we progress through the summer and the second half of 2025. Overall, the market expects a 3.4% year-on-year headline CPI by the end of the year.

PCE Price Index Inflation YoY Dips to 2.1%

The most encouraging data point during May? Implied recession odds. According to online prediction markets, the chance of a technical U.S. recession (two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth) plunged from 70% on April 30 to 33% by May 30.

A much better-than-expected goods trade deficit report on Friday helped support what’s likely to be a 2%+ GDP print for the current quarter.

U.S. Recession Probability Halved

Put it all together, and the Fed is likely to do nothing for the foreseeable future. The effective Fed Funds rate is seen holding at 4.33% at least through July, with just a modest chance of a quarter-point September ease. It will make Chair Powell’s Jackson Hole speech all the more interesting.

That presentation will come after what’s likely to be a series of nudges from President Trump calling on the Fed chief to cut rates sooner rather than later. Powell met with Trump at the White House last Thursday.

Fed Seen Holding Steady Through August

Despite a better macroeconomic backdrop compared to two months ago, strategists keep slashing their current-year S&P 500 EPS forecasts.

Now below $265 (with out-year per-share earnings estimates under $300), the SPX’s price-to-earnings ratio is above 21, not far from its early-year highs.

S&P 500 EPS Trend Remains Lower

Finally, investors should be aware that we are in the throes of bullish seasonality. Readers of my analyses know that I like to shed light on calendar trends, and for the S&P 500 ETF, the period from May to July has been particularly strong.

The ETF, excluding dividends, has been up in 26 of the past 28 months, from May through July. June tends to be a less strong month, averaging a 0.8% price advance.

S&P 500 Leans Bullish Through July

The Bottom Line

The stock market has been resilient since hitting its April 7 post-Liberation Day intrasession low. May was a banner month, with global stocks notching record highs despite the interest rate rise. Economic data has been strong, though the so-called “soft” survey data remains bleak.

Going forward, all eyes will be on President Trump’s Truth Social page and each passing macro data point.

data.title: Solana Price Analysis: Can Bulls Break Above Recent Highs Of $185?
data.description:
By Christian Norman

Year-to-date, 2025 has proven to be an interesting time for the crypto market, with Solana being no exception.

Enjoying a period of bullish momentum bookmarked by Trump’s win in the election, seen as the pro-crypto candidate of choice, Solana not only rose to an all-time high just shy of $300 but overtook Binance Coin (BNB) to become the fifth-largest coin by market capitalization globally.

Currently trading at 179.38, a ~39% discount from all-time highs made in mid-January, the recent fall in Solana pricing has sparked interest amongst those looking for a buying opportunity.

Notwithstanding, this decline in value is not exclusive to Solana, with much of the crypto space feeling the full force of waning market risk appetite.

With a meteoric rise in crypto value still fresh in the collective memory, the $1,000,000 question becomes whether this phenomenon will continue in 2025, or whether general market risk aversion will bode negatively for crypto— Solana included.

What’s next for Solana?

SOLUSD: Technical analysis

  • Moving averages: Currently, long-term moving averages such as the 100 and 200-period suggest bullish directional bias, with recent price action breaking above key levels of support. This suggests that Solana has found sustained buyer pressure in the medium to long-term, and should remain in a general uptrend should current momentum continue.
  • Oscillators lean neutral to sell: Many of the most commonly used trading indicators currently lean towards either a neutral or bearish bias. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) and Stochastics currently or recently have Solana as ‘overbought.’ At the same time, the MACD shows a weakening bull trend, with the distance between the MACD and signal line narrowing. Increased US rate cut bets are also benefiting silver pricing, with markets increasingly anticipating two interest rate cuts in 2025
  • Key levels: At the time of writing, Solana trades rangebound between ~$165 and ~$184. Historically, this is a range where bull trends are either made or broken. A sustained break above this level would suggest further bullish momentum, while the opposite could impose further downside from January’s highs.

SOLUSD: Fundamental analysis

  • Surge in DEX trading volume: Recent data has shown a significant increase in Solana’s weekly decentralized trading volume (DEX), outperforming many other large-cap crypto coins. This growth in volume suggests higher levels of user activity, liquidity, and growing utility for the Solana network, which is encouraging for Solana in the medium to long term.
  • Crypto strategic reserve: A pledge by Donald Trump as part of his 2024 campaign, Solana is included in the US government’s “Digital Asset Stockpile”. Once a crypto-sceptic, Trump has not only overseen the creation of a United States crypto reserve but also released his project on the Solana blockchain - the now-infamous $TRUMP coin. Although some, including Solana’s co-founder, have reservations about governmental endorsement of crypto, the short-term effects on Solana pricing have historically been positive.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Helmerich & Payne: Best-In-Class Driller Trading At Trough Multiples
data.description:
Summary

Helmerich & Payne (NYSE:HP) is one of the world’s largest onshore drilling contractors. It owns and operates industry leading rigs. The company has been penalized heavily for both macro and company-related reasons such as reducing global active rigs, one-off integration cost and Saudi rig suspensions. However, there are still many reasons to be bullish about the company such as its strong backlog, future synergies savings, best-in-class high margin rigs and depressed multiples. Therefore, buying a small position at this level when the market is fearful while getting a decent dividend yield is not a bad idea.

Business Model

Essentially, HP owns the best-in-class drilling rigs. As Exploration & Production firms like Shell and BP need to drill oil and gas wells, they would rent its rigs for a daily fee which is called Day Rates as they do not want these assets on their balance sheet. Moreover, HP also provides performance-based contracts for their rigs. This means that they can earn an extra ~$1-2k/day (find in page 10) for renting out their drillers if they can drill wells more efficiently. Thus, HP would be incentivized to upgrade their tools in analytics capabilities and technology to reduce cost while increasing profitability. H&P also sells software tools and after market services through a recurring subscription plan to offset some cyclicality in their drilling revenue. Lastly, they also have an asset-light offshore drilling business, where they provide their services for a fixed fee.

HP is the largest driller in the US, leading in both margins and market share. In fact, its margins are becoming increasingly higher than its peers over the years, and they are ~40% higher (find in page 8 of Q2 2025 earnings report). This is mainly driven by its FlexRigs which are built for performance where they can handle faster drilling and lower maintenance cost. Moreover, 50% of its North America contracts are performance-based, allowing HP to earn more for its efficiency, compared to its peers which have no upside for efficiency gain.

As these rigs generate cash, HP is able to return cash to shareholders through dividends while paying down its acquisition debt. Therefore, HP buys and operates efficient drilling rigs, ensuring good performance. Then the cash received is used to allocate to better technology and rewarding shareholders.

Reviewing Q2 2025

HP delivered a slight beat in terms of revenue by $60.6mil and a miss in EPS due to one-off transaction charges and tax lumpiness. However, its segment results are very healthy. The North America solutions segment exceeded the guidance range and contributed directly to the margin of $266mil. Secondly, its International solutions segment contributed a direct margin of $27mil from a $7mil loss in Q1 despite suspensions, well above its guidance. Lastly, its offshore rigs produced $26mil of direct margin, well within the upper range of guidance.

Therefore, all three segments had positive results and that led to ~$222 EBITDA and positive cash flow from operations. In fact, management is bullish on long-term fundamentals especially with their impressive backlog of $7.6bn, superior margins for further growth.

Valuation and Investment Thesis

HP is currently trading at a deeply depressed level relative to both historical norms and the sector median of its peers. What is even more striking is its TTM EV/EBITDA and Price/Sales are trading at an 81% and 73% discount from its 5-year average respectively.

I think this huge valuation gap reflects market concern over the entire sector, especially with falling overall rig counts. According to Baker Huges, the North American land rigs fell to 566 rigs from 600 rigs since last year. This means there is lower demand which would flow to lesser revenue overall. An under-utilized fleet would also mean margin compression as the fixed cost fleet is spread over lesser operating rigs. Since last year, its peer’s day-rates have declined ~7% (find in page 8 of Q2 2025 earnings report). Of course, this has also led to a decline in free cash flow. Therefore, these factors have led to very negative sentiment, and its share price has reduced back to 2020 Covid lows.

However, 50% of HP North American fleet is under performance contracts which have higher-margins, and they could also mothball lower-spec fleets while keeping the higher margin fleet active. In Q2, HP’s cashflow reduced to $56M due to one-off acquisition costs, which compressed free cash flow. However, these charges are non-recurring and HP expects $50-70M annual cost savings synergies which would lead to lower rig cost moving forward.

Also, HP has many enduring strengths that the market is not appreciating as much, such as its massive backlog of $7.6bn which provides multi-year revenue visibility. Moreover, its dividend yield of 6.9% is also higher than the sector median with its payout ratio below 50% for FY24.

With success in the North America performance-based contracts, HP could also broker some of its contracted rigs in Mexico and Brazil to be under the performance-based contracts as well. This would yield incremental EBITDA, without additional capital expenditure. This could happen because management is finding new avenues to grow internationally, and it would make logical sense to copy what worked and use the same model elsewhere.

Risk

In every energy company, the risk is tied to falling oil prices. As oil prices serve as a function of demand and when oil prices fall too low, there would be less incentive for E&P companies to borrow drilling rigs from HP and that may lead to day rates compression and even lesser contract revenue.

Management also has no clear view when the Saudi Rigs would be restarted and for good reasons. Depressed oil prices have reduced drilling economics and prompting Aramco to defer rig deployment until a sustained price recovery to trigger capital spend commitments.

Conclusion

Despite near term headwinds in US rig count drops, integration costs and Saudi rig suspensions, I think the market has already priced in this information. Also, HP’s top tier margins, strong backlog and decent dividend yield remain intact. With possible expanded performance-contracts and depressed multiples, I think there is good upside when the cycle turns. Thus, I think buying a small position now is not a bad idea.

data.title: Roku: Underappreciated Growth Drivers As Profit Margins Expand
data.description:
To say that the market right now is jittery would be an understatement. The major indices are swerving wildly on the latest trade developments. It's a stock-pickers' market that rewards careful stock selection, but underneath the headline noise, long-term investors should still take comfort in buying strong assets at a reasonable price.

Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU), in my view, is one of these stocks. The platform streaming company continues to grow its base of subscribers, while a new refresh of its hardware lineup has modernized the brand and increased its relevance especially against higher-priced premium rivals. Roku has rallied ~30% from its post-tariff peaks, but the stock is still only ~flat for the year and has plenty of upside as the company continues to pad its profitability margins.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Roku in March, when the stock was trading near $80 per share. Since then, Roku stock has corrected somewhat, while the company has continued to deliver strong platform revenue growth. Amid a spate of newly released products, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Roku has just refreshed almost the entirety of its device lineup. It has a new Roku Select (starting at ~$220 for a 43" model) and Plus Series TVs (starting at $349 for a 55" model), while also introducing the company's new streaming sticks ($29.99 for a base model, $39.99 for a 4K version).

Roku is far from merely a hardware company now: but the refresh of its low-cost streaming sticks makes the company more relevant against key competitors like Amazon's Fire Stick (similarly priced) and Apple's AppleTV (at a premium price).

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on Roku:

Roku benefits from recent streaming service price increases.

On top of generating advertising revenue from its own ad inventory, Roku also takes a slice of subscription revenue that is billed through Roku TV. The company's increased proliferation of low-cost streaming devices (at an accessible price even for 4K streaming options) has increased the company's market share). As streamers like Netflix (NFLX) continue to lead the pack in pushing monthly subscriptions higher, Roku also has a built-in revenue increase.

Roku is still enjoying secular tailwinds toward streaming.

Cord-cutting is still on the rise, especially as Disney (DIS) releases a new standalone ESPN streaming offering, severing one of the last major links that cable subscribers have to their legacy packages. While the streaming companies are locked in a price and content war against each other, Roku benefits from the overall shift into streaming no matter which streaming service comes out on top.

Building profitability.

Roku is starting to recognize meaningful economies of scale on a business that continues to tilt toward higher-margin streaming/platform revenue. The company has achieved consistent adjusted EBITDA margin expansion as opex grows slower than revenue.

There's a lot to like about Roku, especially after the recent post-earnings slide. Buy here and hold out for a broader rebound.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Roku's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Roku's revenue grew 16% y/y to $1.021 billion, coming in ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $1.008 billion (+14% y/y) by a three-point margin. Platform revenue, which now represents nearly 90% of Roku's total, grew at a 17% y/y pace, decelerating only two points from Q4's 19% y/y pace.

We note that Roku announced its device refresh in late April, so end customer sell-through of the new devices (plus the platform revenue accruing from any new-to-Roku users acquired from these devices) has not yet benefited Roku's results. The company's outlook for Q2 calls for $1.07 billion in revenue, which indicates a slowdown to 11% y/y overall growth: which appears conservative if the new device launch is able to bring in a fold of new buyers.

So far, the company notes that macroeconomic headwinds have not yet impacted the company's sales momentum, concern over which has weighed particularly for the company's advertising revenue streams. On the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call, Roku CFO Dan Jedda noted that even if marketing budgets begin to tighten, Roku has an advantage over analog/traditional media spending in that it is able to provide and measure clear ROI for marketers. In other words, in an environment where advertisers are looking to wring maximum value out of every dollar spent, Roku has an advantage in being able to track that performance. Per Jedda's remarks on the earnings call:

We've talked a lot about advertising and new products and the new initiatives, the growth of our supply. And now we're talking a lot more about subscriptions with the acquisition of Frndly and many more initiatives we have yet to announce.

So, I think some of these initiatives, we're going to keep doing new things and these initiatives are going to play out over time. And so I do think like in the back half of the year, some of the -- if there is a greater macro environment, some of our new initiatives should help to offset that.

Obviously, we're not immune if there is a major macro environment recession type, especially specifically in the ad market. But we also have tremendous secular tailwinds as the market continues to shift from linear to digital. And now the shift that Charlie just talked about from guaranteed to non-guaranteed, we're also very well positioned.

One thing I'll just add on this is, advertisers are asking more for ROI and measurement. That is a space we play very well in, given all the initiatives we have to increase demand to our Platform because we have the supply and increase our ability to measure that demand. These are positive tailwinds for us. And so there's a lot of positives going on now and there'll be more that that we discuss in the future."

Consistent with Roku's strategy to bring in more viewers and subscribers via a low barrier to entry, the company's hardware gross margins declined 9 points y/y to -13%, which was partially offset by a 50bps improvement in platform gross margins. We note as well that Roku has affirmed that the current tariff regime will not have any material impact on the company's gross margin profile.

And in spite of device margin headwinds, Roku still managed to expand its adjusted EBITDA margins by 90bps y/y to 5.5%, primarily by keeping a lid on R&D expenses to just 2% y/y growth. Nominal adjusted EBITDA dollars, meanwhile, grew 37% y/y. Roku is in a sweet spot in which its product refreshes don't require significant investments, while growth in devices revenue affirms that the company is still managing to stay relevant via an attractive price point and bringing in new subscribers to the Roku platform.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

At current share prices near $73, Roku trades at a market cap of $10.68 billion. After we net off the $2.26 billion of cash (against zero debt, which is another reason to be long on Roku), the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.42 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Roku has maintained its full-year outlook of $4.55 billion in revenue (+12% y/y) and $350 million in adjusted EBITDA, which represents a 7.7% adjusted EBITDA margin (with guidance for Q2 also implying that adjusted EBITDA margins will continue to improve beyond their current 5.5% levels as we move throughout the year, which is a healthy indicator in a tough and tariff-dominated environment).

This positions Roku's valuation at:

  • 1.9x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 24x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

To me, Roku still sits at very buyable levels: especially when we consider the fact that its mid-20s adjusted EBITDA multiple is set against >30% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA. Roku is still in the earlier phases of monetizing its growing user base (125 million people land on the Roku Home Screen every day) and achieving economies of scale to drive adjusted EBITDA higher.

Of course, there are risks that investors should keep in mind. As previously mentioned, a tougher consumer spending macro may drive marketers to push down their advertising budgets (though Roku has noted that its ability to track and report marketing ROI may allow it to gain market share against other advertising channels). Consumers pinching their budgets could also limit the number of streaming services they subscribe to, hurting Roku's affiliate revenue.

That being said, I see far more positives than risks for Roku, as the company benefits from secular streaming tailwinds and will grow regardless of which streaming powerhouse becomes more prominent. The company's consistent boost in EBITDA margins is a reflection of its commitment to growing efficiently. Stay long here.

data.title: Full Truck Alliance: Strong Beat, But Valuation Calls For Patience
data.description:
Full Truck Alliance (NYSE:YMM) reported a strong set of results with revenue and non-GAAP earnings beating consensus expectations. The largely in line Q2 revenue guidance also gives investors comfort in the sustainability of the revenue growth profile and that Full Truck Alliance remains the leading logistics platform in China. Management guided solid fulfilled order volume in April and May, implying that the Q2 estimate is at least achievable, if not a positive surprise.

We like Full Truck Alliance given that its dominant position in China is unlikely to be challenged anytime soon, and that the investment in AI and Plus PRC will likely lead to long-term tech innovation and further strengthen YMM’s competitive moat. However, we believe that the current valuation of 5x forward revenue multiple already reflect this bullish sentiment and that much of the forward expectations have been priced in.

We will need to see further execution on the operating metrics in Q2 and Q3 to be more confident in the stock and recommend investors to stay on the sidelines for now, hence our neutral rating on the stock.

Solid all-around execution

Total revenue grew 19% y/y to RMB 2.7 billion, which came in roughly 2% ahead of consensus, while non-GAAP net income of 1.3 billion increased 84% y/y, which also came above consensus. The company finished the quarter with 2.76 million average monthly shippers, a 29% y/y growth that drove 23% y/y growth in fulfilled order volume to 48.2 million.

Notably, transaction service revenue was a standout, growing 51.5% y/y to RMB 1.05 billion, which was well ahead of the consensus 45% growth estimate. The strength was largely driven by higher order volume and improved monetization that underscores Full Truck Alliance’s scalability and pricing power.

Freight brokerage service revenue remained stable at RMB 966 million, ahead of consensus, while listing service revenue grew a healthy 10% y/y to RMB 235 million. Finally, VAS posted solid growth of 13.5% to RMB 435 million, driven by increasing demand for credit and other supplementary solutions.

Strength in both operating metrics drove the growth in topline results, which is leading to improvement in operating leverage with GPM% increased by 19.6ppt to 74%, well ahead of the consensus 70% estimate and leading to non-GAAP net margin of 51% vs. the consensus 48%.

Management guided Q2 revenue growth between 10.6% and 12.9%, the midpoint of which is largely in line with consensus. Given that management cited positive April and May trends, we believe Q2 could see another upside surprise in consensus estimates if June volume holds up.

A growing network effect

Management cited that total shipper MAUs surpassed 3 million in March and set a new monthly record, underscoring Full Truck Alliance’s network effect within the logistics industry.

Total shipper members exceeded 1.1 million at the end of Q1 with the 12-month rolling retention rate for shipper members remained above 80%. Management also highlighted that order contribution from direct shippers increased by 51% in the Q with average fulfillment rate of low and medium frequency direct shippers reaching 60%, a roughly 10ppt improvement from a year ago.

The platform's overall fulfillment rate increased 6ppt to 39.2%, which is a record high, driven by a growing number of truckers fulfilling orders through the Full Truck Alliance platform over the past year to 4.18 million. More importantly, the next month's retention rate for truckers responding to new orders has consistently stayed above 85%, further reinforcing Full Truck Alliance’s platform stickiness and network effect.

Further Commercialization Efforts To Drive Higher Revenue Sustainability

Full Truck Alliance has expanded its commission service to 39 additional cities across Northeast and Southwest China as part of its ongoing commercialization efforts. This model involves requiring truckers to pay a deposit to access shipper contact information and place bids on shipments. Once a shipment is awarded, Full Truck Alliance deducts a commission fee ranging from 10% to 15% from the deposit before returning the balance to the trucker.

The expansion into Northeast and Southwest cities is part of the company's broader strategy of driving higher commission service revenue, which saw 52% y/y growth in Q1.

We note that these two regional expansions increased Full Truck Alliance’s commission coverage to 85% from 77% last year, and the transition towards a more refined order-based model is likely to lead to a higher monetization rate while enhancing user experience.

Management pointed out that monetization per order increased 12% y/y to RMB 25.5 in the Q vs. RMB 22.7 a year ago due to higher quality transactions. We believe there is sustainability to this trend as Full Truck Alliance continues to improve freight pricing and price protection for the truckers, leading to further upside to commission revenue.

Plus PRC Investment Is A Long-term Positive

In May 16, Full Truck Alliance’s board approved an additional $125 million investment in Plus PRC, a leading autonomous driving company in China. This investment is based on the principal and accrued interest of the convertible note issued by Plus PRC in May 2024 and January of this year. Post the completion of the transaction, Full Truck Alliance will have 52.8% of equity interest and 56.2% of voting rights.

We believe this investment in strategically beneficial to the company in several ways.

First, Full Truck Alliance gains a technological moat in autonomous trucking, which allows it to gradually pivot into having its own logistics fleet of autonomous heavy-duty trucks.

Second, the integration of Plus PRC technology into trucks and shippers allows Full Truck Alliance to achieve a better balance on supply and demand, properly allocating trucks and shippers on the supply side to meet any outsized move in demand.

Finally, Full Truck Alliance could be building a more competitive AI and autonomous driving ecosystem within heavy-duty logistics. We believe that the era of autonomous driving has reached an inflection point in which autonomous heavy-duty trucks are already being used in mines to perform logistics duties, and it is a matter of time before autonomous trucks hit China’s roads. Full Truck Alliance will be well-positioned once regulations are in place for the commercialization of this technology on a greater scale.

Valuation Appears Full, Wait For A Better Entry Point

Full Truck Alliance currently trades at 4.8x forward revenue, which is a premium compared with its domestic order aggregator platforms, such as Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGY) and Didi (OTCPK:DIDIY), that trade around 1x, and US peers, such as Uber (UBER) and Dash (DASH), that trade between 3x and 6x.

We believe that Full Truck Alliance deserves a premium valuation given it is the largest player in China with no clear number two. However, we also believe that the current valuation seems to be demanding, and we believe investors can wait for a better entry point.

The catalyst for us to upgrade our view includes solid execution in Q2 followed by strong Q3 guidance. Note that as we head later into the year, China’s macro will likely see considerable headwinds on the back of tariff uncertainties and lack of bazooka stimulus, which will likely impact trucking and logistics demand. If the macro environment gradually improves with demand coming in strong in the second half, then we would be more bullish on the stock. Otherwise, we remain on the sidelines for now.

data.title: The PCE Moves Closer To The Fed's Target, But Non Tariff Threats Remain
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Economic Analysis released the personal consumption expenditures report for the month of April. The PCE report is one of the most popular tools for measuring inflation and is used by the Federal Reserve. The report saw overall inflation increase by 0.1% during the month of April and by 2.1% on a year-over-year basis. When removing food and energy from the equation, core PCE also rose by 0.1% for the month and 2.5% year over year. After stalling throughout most of 2024, disinflation appears to have resumed, but the effects of the tariffs continue to create uncertainty towards the future.

Service Sector Drives Inflation, But Makes Huge Improvement

While the initial surge in inflation during the pandemic was driven by supply shortages in goods, the source of the economy’s current inflation situation lies within the service sector. Goods inflation inched higher by 0.1% in the month of April but remains at a deflationary pace on a year-over-year basis at -0.4%. Goods inflation has been at or near deflationary levels dating back to the middle of 2023.

Despite its recent history of being the inflationary driver, inflation in the service sector rose to only 0.1% as well in the month of April, the lowest monthly increase in more than three years. On a year-over-year basis, service sector inflation rose by 3.3%, also the lowest level in more than three years. The moderation of service sector inflation has been key to getting inflation closer to the Federal Reserve’s long-range goal.

Goods Inflation and Tariffs - What We Know So Far

While the ongoing uncertainty surrounding trade policy could move prices upwards, there appears to be no evidence to suggest that is happening yet. In fact, the deflationary pressures in the goods sector seem to be equally spread between durable and nondurable goods. March saw price declines at or greater than 0.5% for each. It is important to note that recent data may be slightly skewed by the rush of imports that came in prior to the inauguration and liberation day.

Areas to Watch in the Service Sector - Housing, Healthcare, and Financial Services

While service sector inflation appears to be moderating, that may not be the case across the board and certain sectors within the space should continue to be monitored for signs of pushing inflation higher. Housing would be the biggest area of concern here, as housing and utilities inflation over the last two months have come in at their highest levels in over a year. Additionally, year over year housing inflation is beginning to tick back up, now at 4.4%

Another area of concern is healthcare. Like housing, two of the three highest monthly inflation indicators have been recorded over the last three months. Healthcare is becoming a larger part of the consumer’s spending profile, and while the sector has had stable inflation over the past three years, the year-over-year change has reverted sharply higher to 2.5% in the last few months.

The last service sector to keep an eye on is financial services and insurance, which increased by more than 1.0% in February and decreased by more than 1.0% in April. The sector is prone to volatile swings in pricing and while the year over year inflationary rate briefly touched 7% late last year, it is currently down to below 5%. A sharp reversal upwards, combined with continued headwinds in healthcare and housing, could be strong enough to send inflation higher and away from the 2% goal.

What’s Next for the Federal Reserve?

Since core PCE is the Federal Reserve’s primary measure of inflation, the index remains 0.5% above the Central Bank’s long-term target, but is 0.3% below the 2025 expectation. Meanwhile, unemployment remains firmly at the Fed’s long-range target (and better than short-term expectations), which gives the Central Bank wiggle room to continue to pause rate cuts until it sees inflation come down further.

Conclusion

The tariffs have yet to create an inflationary impact on the economy, but the uncertainty surrounding their outcome will likely have the Federal Reserve desiring to continue to hold. The pace at which inflation is declining has improved, but a deflationary threat (which would warrant rate cuts), remains remote. I’m expecting the Fed to tighten its rate outlook for 2025 in its June meeting, along with lowering inflation expectations.

data.title: Social Media Internationalization Make Hello Group Very Cheap
data.description:
Hello Group Inc. (NASDAQ:MOMO), the social network from China, is now targeting high-return paying users, which may lower the total number of users, but accelerate future FCF numbers. Management also announced that it is expecting the company's international expansion to enhance future financial figures. Considering the MOMO's connections with a large network of broadcasters and the expectations of the social media market in China, I believe that we could see further free cash flow generation in the coming years. Even considering potential risks from changes in the regulations in China or competitions for broadcasters, MOMO trades substantially undervalued. In my opinion, the stock is a buy.

MOMO And The Outlook

MOMO is one of the largest players in China's online social networking space. MOMO appears to be the third-largest mobile app, after WeChat and QQ. MOMO connects users based on their location and interests. The company noted the introduction of a number of new apps like Hertz, Soulchill, and Duidui, which, I believe, could accelerate growth in the coming years.

The company offers live video services, which represented $697 million in 2024. MOMO's value-added services stood at $729 million in 2024. According to the last annual report, value-added services are membership subscriptions and virtual gift services. Besides, the company offers mobile games, and other services. I obtained the table below from the most recent quarterly report.

In my view, the best description about the MOMO's platform is from the company's website. The company also offers the Tantan app, which is a dating application, and other apps. However, I do not believe those apps are very profitable right now.

We connect people and facilitate interactions over a rich collection of social experiences based on location, interests, content sharing and a variety of recreational activities including live talent shows, short videos, social games as well as other video- and audio-based interactive experiences, such as online parties, mobile karaoke and user participated reality shows. Source: IR

MOMO does not seem to be that optimistic about the numbers to be delivered in 2025. In Q1 2025, management expected a decline of 6.3%-2.4% year over year. In my opinion, the year 2025 may not be a beneficial time period. In my view, it may the right time to buy shares.

For the first quarter of 2025, the Company expects total net revenues to be between RMB2.4 billion to RMB2.5 billion, representing a decrease of 6.3% to 2.4% year over year. This forecast reflects the Company's current and preliminary views on the market and operational conditions, which are subject to change. Source: Quarterly Press Release

The company is currently trading at 3.99x FWD EBITDA, 0.62x book value, and 6.97x FWD GAAP earnings. Besides, MOMO trades at 5.14x 2027 earnings. Yes, the company recently reported lower-than-expected EPS. However, in my view, it looks like a Buy at the current price mark.

In June, MOMO is expected to deliver quarterly revenue of $335 million, and EPS GAAP of $0.17 per share. From 2020 to 2024, the company reported better-than-expected EPS results on many occasions. If the EPS figure is better than expected, in my view, the stock price could trend north.

International expansion could bring substantial revenue growth; reduction in low-return paying users may contribute too

MOMO appears to be making significant efforts to invest in international markets with strategies that worked in China. Given MOMO's success in China, I would expect similar results elsewhere. In this regard, the company made several comments in the last earnings call. MOMO expects growth in international markets as well as an improvement in profitability thanks to significant reductions in the number of low-return paying users. MOMO appears to be targeting only paid users and users that pay significant amount of money. As a result, we could expect a decline in total users, but improvements in free cash flow growth.

The company anticipates continued growth in international markets, supported by localization strategies and operational improvements. Source: Earnings Call

COO Zhang Sichuan explained the reduction of efforts to acquire low-return paying users, which led to a drop in paying users but improved profitability. Source: Earnings Call

Repurchases represent a stock catalyst

In 2025, the company repurchased a number of ADSs in the open market. Besides, MOMO acquired a large number of shares in the most recent history. According to Seeking Alpha, the share count declined from 206 million in 2020 to 164.1 million in 2024, which shows that management is quite concerned about the valuation of the company. I believe that further acquisition of shares could enhance the implied valuation of the shares.

As of March 12, 2025, the Company has repurchased 43.5 million ADSs for US$264.0 million on the open market under Share Repurchase Programannounced on June 7, 2022 and amended on March 14, 2024, at an average purchase price of US$6.05 per ADS. Source: 6-k

Broadcasters, negotiation, and competition

The company claims to have a pool of talented broadcasters and talent agencies. After operating for many years in the same industry, in my view, the contacts with broadcasters make MOMO's business model strong. In the future, if the company knows how to promote and pay broadcasters, their popularity will most likely bring visitors, revenue growth, and free cash flow growth. With that being said, the negotiations with broadcasters and talent agencies may also fail. If other competitors decide to pay more for content, or the new content offered is not appreciated, the numbers of users could decline, which may lead to a decline in the value of MOMO's stock.

MOMO appears to be making more money, but the user base recently declined

I reviewed the company's free cash flow in the most recent history. MOMO appears to be making much more money right now than a few years ago. With that, at the same time, the user growth did not increase, which is quite worrying. If users decide that the content offered is not any more interesting, they may leave the platform. Analysts out there could issue warnings about the user base decrease, and shareholders could also decide to sell their shares. The stock price may continue to decline. The company made several comments about these warnings in the last annual report.

If our user growth rate remains negative, our success will become increasingly dependent on our ability to retain existing users and enhance user engagement on our platform. If our Momo and Tantan mobile applications are no longer one of the social networking tools that people frequently use, or if people do not perceive our services to be interesting or useful, we may not be able to attract users or increase the frequency or degree of their engagement. Source: 20-F

Changes in Chinese laws could deteriorate revenue line

The company's operations may be affected by changes in the PRC laws and regulations imposed by the government in China. In the last annual report, the company cited several licenses, including the Value-added Telecommunications Business Operation License for information services via the internet. Furthermore, MOMO also noted that it obtained the Internet Culture Operation License. In the future, the government in China may make these licenses more expensive, or MOMO may not obtain the necessary licenses to operate. Under that case scenario, I would be expecting a large decline in revenue and free cash flow growth. As a result, the company's stock price would decline sharply.

Given the uncertainties of interpretation and implementation of the laws and regulations and the enforcement practice by the government authorities, we may be required to obtain additional licenses, permits, filings or approvals for the functions and services of our platform in the future, and may not be able to maintain or renew our current licenses, permits, filings or approvals. Source: 20-F

MOMO could be delisted

Considering the recent changes in the White House, I would expect new information with regard to auditors needed to have the company's shares listed in the United States. According to information from the annual report, If MOMO becomes a Commission-Identified Issuer, MOMO may not be able to sell shares in the exchanges in the United States. As a result, I would expect a substantial decline in the stock price. MOMO could sell shares in China or Europe. However, the company may receive less money from equity investors. Without liquidity, perhaps the investment in the development of new social media apps would not accelerate. As a result, we could see a decline in net income growth.

If the PCAOB determines in the future that it no longer has full access to inspect and investigate completely accounting firms in certain jurisdictions and we continue to use an accounting firm headquartered in one of those jurisdictions to issue an audit report on our financial statements to be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, we would be identified as a Commission-Identified Issuer following the filing of the annual report on Form 20-F for the relevant fiscal year. There can be no assurance that we would not be identified as a Commission-Identified Issuer for any future fiscal year, and if we were so identified for two consecutive years, we would become subject to the prohibition on trading under the HFCAA. Source: 20-F

Valuation: MOMO Looks Quite Cheap

In order to make an assessment of the valuation of MOMO, I reviewed the company's previous free cash flow, net income, and FCF. The average unlevered free cash flow from 2017 to 2023 is close to $217 million, and the average net income stands at close to $175 million.

  • Average Net Income From 2017 to 2023: $175 million
  • Average CFO From 2017 to 2023: $347.16 million
  • Average FCF From 2017 to 2023: $271.06 million

I also included assumptions with regard to the internationalization efforts, and the decline in the number of users. Besides, I studied carefully the expectations about the social media market. The social media market is expected to grow at close to 26.3% from 2025 to 2030, which could be a catalyst for MOMO's revenue growth. The IMF expects China to grow at about 3.8% from 2025 to 2030. I took into consideration these figures in my free cash flow expectations.

The China social media management market generated a revenue of USD 2,188.2 million in 2024 and is expected to reach USD 8,741.2 million by 2030. The China market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 26.3% from 2025 to 2030. Source: Grand View Research

We project China’s potential growth over the medium to long term, showing that potential growth could slow to around 3.8 percent on average between 2025-30 and to around 2.8 percent on average over 2031-40 in the absence of major reforms. Source: IMF

I assumed constant free cash flow from 2025 to 2035 of about $271 million, WACC of 10%, and a terminal EV/ 2035 FCF of 5x. The results obtained included a fair valuation of $15-$16 per share with an enterprise value of $2.3 billion, net present value of future FCF of $1.7 billion, and equity value of $2.5 billion.

My Final Words

The company's new apps Hertz, Soulchill, and Duidui as well as new international efforts could accelerate future free cash flow growth in the coming quarters. MOMO appears to be targeting rich users, and investing a bit less in low-return paying users. The new strategies may not bring many new users, but could be very beneficial for future free cash flow growth. There are a number of risks out there. Under the worst-case scenario, the company may not be able to trade in the United States. With that being said, MOMO could be a very profitable stock in the coming years. As soon as new investors have a look at the company's ability to generate free cash flow, the stock price could trend north.

data.title: Bitcoin Vs. Ethereum In 2025: Comparison And Outlook
data.description:
Explore the key differences of bitcoin and Ethereum in our comprehensive guide. Understand the underlying technology, and how each fits within the market space.

Bitcoin vs Ethereum: Understanding the Key Differences and Similarities

In the rapidly evolving world of digital finance, two names consistently stand out: Bitcoin and Ethereum. These blockchain-based giants are not just cryptocurrencies; they represent the forefront of a financial evolution. Over the years, both have grown from niche experiments into major assets influencing global markets. While Bitcoin and Ethereum share the common ground of being blockchain-based cryptocurrencies, their differences are significant. Bitcoin, the original cryptocurrency, was created as a decentralized digital currency and potential store of value. Meanwhile, Ethereum is a programmable platform that enables smart contracts and decentralized applications (Dapps) to be built and operated without downtime, fraud, or interference.

  • Bitcoin: Often compared to digital gold, it is designed primarily for secure value transfer and storage.
  • Ethereum: A decentralized software platform powering an ecosystem of applications beyond currency, from finance to gaming – via smart contracts.

Both assets have matured significantly, capturing headlines and investor interest worldwide. Below, we explore their origins, technical makeup, market performance, use cases, and what the future may hold for each.

The Growing Interest in Bitcoin and Ethereum

Bitcoin and Ethereum's rising popularity signals more than a trend; it marks crypto's deeper integration into mainstream finance. Often referred to as digital gold, Bitcoin appeals to retail and institutional investors as a potential store of value. Ethereum's smart contracts expand blockchain's role into areas like DeFi, gaming, and decentralized governance.

By 2024, interest surged further. Major financial firms began offering crypto products, and U.S. regulators approved the first Bitcoin and Ethereum ETPs, fueling institutional inflows. The growing overlap between Wall Street and crypto highlights how digital assets have moved from the margins to the financial mainstream.

Digital Market Dynamics: Bitcoin and Ethereum

Bitcoin and Ethereum have reshaped digital markets, advancing crypto adoption for investment and innovation. Bitcoin's decentralized design and recognition make it a key entry point into digital assets. Ethereum powers Web3, a blockchain-based internet layer that enables decentralized apps in finance, social media, and gaming.

Macroeconomic forces now heavily influence both assets. Bitcoin is seen as an inflation hedge, while Ethereum's on-chain ecosystem ties it to market liquidity and tech trends. They're discussed not just in crypto circles but in central bank briefings and institutional portfolios.

Differing Philosophies and Technological Underpinnings

The philosophical and technological foundations of Bitcoin and Ethereum differ significantly. Bitcoin emphasizes decentralization, security, and digital scarcity, serving as a ledger for peer-to-peer value transfer. Ethereum expands on this by enabling programmable contracts and a decentralized world computer.

Consensus Mechanism:

Bitcoin uses Proof-of-Work (PoW), where miners secure the network through computational energy, prioritizing security, but with high energy costs. Ethereum shifted to Proof-of-Stake (PoS) in 2022's "Merge," where validators stake Ether, cutting energy use by over 99% and boosting scalability. The two networks reflect divergent paths: Bitcoin reinforcing PoW's reliability and Ethereum optimizing for sustainability.

Economic Model:

Bitcoin enforces a hard cap of 21 million BTC, supporting its store-of-value status, most recently reinforced by the 2024 halving. Ethereum has no fixed cap; it issues Ether to validators but burns fees (via EIP-1559), allowing for dynamic supply and occasional deflation. Bitcoin follows strict scarcity; Ethereum opts for flexible utility.

Use and Functionality:

Bitcoin is built for secure, straightforward transactions, with limited scripting to ensure reliability and minimal risk. Ethereum's Turing-complete platform enables complex applications from DeFi to digital collectibles—bitcoin champions censorship-resistant money and Ethereum censorship-resistant applications.

Despite these contrasts, both networks continue to evolve along their respective paths—Bitcoin as a secure monetary asset and Ethereum as a versatile innovation platform. Yet both remain grounded in decentralization and open development.

The Genesis of Bitcoin and Ethereum

The inception of Bitcoin and Ethereum marked significant milestones in the evolution of digital finance, introducing the world to the concepts of blockchain and decentralized finance (DeFi). These technologies have since revolutionized how we perceive money, investment, and the internet's architecture, laying the groundwork for a more transparent, secure, and accessible financial system.

Bitcoin Origins

Bitcoin, introduced in a 2008 white paper by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto and launched in January 2009, solved the "double-spending" problem without needing a central authority, enabling secure, peer-to-peer digital transactions via a decentralized blockchain.

Initially embraced by cryptography enthusiasts and libertarians, Bitcoin demonstrated that a decentralized network could protect significant value. With a capped supply and energy-intensive creation process, it established itself as a scarce, censorship-resistant asset often compared to digital gold.

Ethereum Origins

Proposed by Vitalik Buterin in 2013 and launched in 2015, Ethereum expanded blockchain's potential by introducing smart contracts, self-executing code that powers decentralized applications (DApps) beyond simple payments.

Unlike Bitcoin's single-purpose design, Ethereum was built as a multipurpose platform, laying the groundwork for programmable money and Web3. Early use cases included DeFi, tokenized assets, and ICOs. Despite launching later, Ethereum quickly became central to the decentralized web, fueled by a dynamic developer community committed to ongoing innovation.

Bitcoin vs Ethereum: A Technical Comparison

Technologically, Bitcoin and Ethereum differ in purpose, consensus, and architecture, each reflecting a distinct vision. Below is a high-level comparison of key technical aspects.

Consensus Mechanism:

Bitcoin uses Proof of Work (PoW), where miners validate transactions by solving cryptographic puzzles. This method is highly secure but energy-intensive. Since 2022's Merge, Ethereum has used Proof of Stake (PoS), where validators stake Ether to propose blocks. PoS cuts energy use and improves scalability, aligning with Ethereum's sustainability goals.

Block Time:

Bitcoin produces a block every ~10 minutes, leading to slower transaction confirmations. Ethereum's PoS design allows blocks every ~12 seconds, enabling more transactions per minute. Both use scaling solutions like Bitcoin's Lightning Network and Ethereum's layer-2 rollups to increase throughput beyond the base layer.

Smart Contracts and DApps:

Bitcoin supports limited scripting, prioritizing security over programmability. With its Turing-complete environment and smart contract support (via Solidity and the EVM), Ethereum enables a wide range of DApps—from DeFi to NFTs. Bitcoin focuses on secure transfers; Ethereum enables programmable logic.

Supply and Issuance:

Bitcoin's supply is capped at 21 million, and mining rewards halve every four years, making it a disinflationary asset. Ethereum has no fixed cap, but since EIP-1559, part of each transaction fee is burned. ETH can become deflationary during high usage, balancing rewards with supply control.

These technical differences reflect core philosophies: Bitcoin is stable and secure for digital money; Ethereum is adaptable and innovative for decentralized services. Both have driven blockchain's evolution in complementary ways.

Key Bitcoin Features

Bitcoin's architecture includes several core features that have fueled its success as the first decentralized digital currency.

Blockchain Ledger:

Bitcoin's blockchain is a decentralized ledger recording all transactions across a global network. Each block contains verified transactions added through a public, tamper-resistant process. This ensures security and independence from central authorities, reducing fraud and censorship risks.

Mining Process:

Bitcoin uses Proof of Work (PoW), where miners solve mathematical puzzles to validate transactions and secure the network. In return, they receive new bitcoins, introducing a predictable issuance schedule.

Transaction Speed and Scalability:

Bitcoin's base layer processes a block every ~10 minutes, handling 5–7 transactions per second. This limited throughput can lead to congestion and high fees. Upgrades like SegWit improved block efficiency, while the Lightning Network, a Layer-2 solution, enables instant, low-cost off-chain payments, which, by 2025, support microtransactions and advanced use cases.

Ongoing Innovations:

While cautious in its evolution, Bitcoin continues to advance. The 2021 Taproot upgrade enhanced privacy and flexibility through Schnorr signatures and Merkelized scripts. Projects like sidechains (Liquid, Rootstock) further expand Bitcoin's capabilities while maintaining its core value of minimizing trust.

Key Ethereum Features

Ethereum's architecture introduces key innovations that extend blockchain utility beyond simple transactions.

Smart Contracts:

Ethereum pioneered smart contracts, a self-executing code that automates agreements without intermediaries. A contract might release a loan when collateral is posted or reclaim it if terms aren't met. This logic powers decentralized finance (DeFi), NFTs, supply chains, and more, enabling transparent, autonomous applications.

Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM):

The EVM runs all smart contracts on Ethereum, acting as a global decentralized computer. Every node computes the same outcome for a given contract, ensuring consensus. Its developer-friendly design has made Ethereum the standard for decentralized app (DAPP) development, with many blockchains adopting EVM compatibility.

Major Upgrades (The Merge and Beyond):

Ethereum's 2022 transition to Proof of Stake (The Merge) slashed energy use and laid the groundwork for further scaling. Future upgrades aim to improve throughput and cost-efficiency while preserving Ethereum's programmability.

Ecosystem and Token Standards:

Ethereum's token standards; ERC-20 for fungible tokens and ERC-721 for NFTs, sparked major crypto trends like ICOs and digital collectibles. Ethereum hosts tokenized assets from stablecoins to securities, reinforcing its role as a flexible platform for digital value creation.

To learn more about how Ethereum works, read VanEck's Ethereum 101: A Beginner's Guide.

Bitcoin and Ethereum Core Differences

Bitcoin and Ethereum's technological frameworks and future scalability plans highlight their unique positions and objectives within the cryptocurrency ecosystem. The chart below highlights the core technical differences between the two cryptocurrencies:

Feature Bitcoin Ethereum
Creator(s) Satoshi Nakamoto Vitalik Buterin, Charles Hoskinson, Gavin Wood, Joseph Lubin, and Anthony Di Iorio
Launch Date January 2009 July 2015
Currency vs. Platform A credible alternative to traditional fiat currencies (medium of exchange, potential store of value) A platform to run programmatic contracts and applications via Ether
Consensus Algorithm Proof-of-Work (PoW) Proof-of-Stake (PoS)
Block Time 10 minutes on average 12 seconds on average
Transaction Throughput 7 transactions per second (TPS) 14 transactions per second (TPS)
Supply Finite supply-capped at 21 million BTC Infinite supply
Scalability Solutions SegWit, Lightning Network Ethereum 2.0, Sharding, Plasma

Technological Frameworks

Bitcoin is purpose-built for secure, decentralized value transfer. Its development is conservative, and changes like SegWit and Taproot are incremental and backward-compatible. Ethereum, by contrast, is a flexible platform designed to evolve. Originally PoW-based, it now runs on PoS and supports smart contracts, with ongoing upgrades proposed via EIPs. Bitcoin favors stability; Ethereum prioritizes adaptability.

Scalability Approach

Bitcoin scales via off-chain solutions like the Lightning Network, preserving its lean base layer and full-node accessibility. Ethereum uses both on-chain upgrades and Layer-2 solutions (e.g., Rollups), with sharding planned to expand base-layer capacity. Bitcoin's model keeps complexity low; Ethereum's boosts throughput with a more dynamic, modular architecture.

Market Performance: Bitcoin Price vs Ethereum

The market performance of Bitcoin and Ethereum provides a fascinating glimpse into the dynamics of cryptocurrency markets. Both have experienced significant price fluctuations over the years, shaped by various factors.

Historical Price Trends

Both Bitcoin and Ethereum saw notable volatility over the past year, but Bitcoin has clearly outperformed. Following the April 2024 halving, Bitcoin's price showed resilience, rising ~16% through March 2025, while Ethereum dropped ~50%. This changed in the past week as Ethereum rose over 50%. The halving and the launch of U.S. spot Bitcoin ETPs drew investor capital toward BTC and away from ETH.

Ethereum has seen dramatic swings in recent months, while Bitcoin remained more stable.

Part of this disparity stems from Bitcoin's singular role as a potential store of value, which appeals in uncertain markets. Ethereum, though critical to DeFi and smart contracts, has faced unique headwinds that continue to shape its outlook.

Factors Influencing Price

Several key factors influence the price of Bitcoin and Ethereum, including market demand, technological upgrades, and investor sentiment.

Market Demand & Adoption:

Bitcoin benefits from its reputation, gaining traction as a hedge against inflation and monetary instability, particularly during periods of economic uncertainty. Ethereum's demand is tied to its utility, with spikes in DeFi, NFTs, or broader blockchain use driving ETH usage and price. By 2025, rising global adoption from individuals to governments has contributed to the growing demand for both assets.

Technological Upgrades & Innovations:

Major protocol upgrades often influence prices. Bitcoin's halving events reduce new supply and have historically preceded bull runs. Technical improvements like SegWit or growing Lightning Network adoption also boost confidence. For Ethereum, milestones like The Merge and upcoming sharding plans increase scalability and reduce supply growth, often triggering positive sentiment. These upgrades enhance utility and signal progress, which can attract new investors.

Investor Sentiment & Macro Trends:

Sentiments, shaped by news, institutional moves, or macro events, have a strong short-term impact. ETP approvals, large corporate investments, or regulatory clarity can spark rallies. Conversely, hacks, restrictions, or economic fear can drive selloffs. Bitcoin often benefits from inflation concerns or distrust in fiat systems, while Ethereum gains when innovation in its ecosystem is strong.

Bitcoin vs Ethereum Performance

Practical Uses: From Everyday Transactions to Smart Contracts

Bitcoin and Ethereum differ in primary use cases, shaped by their core design philosophies.

Bitcoin's Uses:

Bitcoin functions as both digital money and a store of value. It's used for cross-border payments and remittances and as an alternative currency in regions with unstable economies. With tools like the Lightning Network, Bitcoin now supports fast, low-cost micro-transactions. However, by 2025, its dominant role will be as a potential store of value, a hedge against inflation held by investors, companies, and even nation-states. Its finite supply and resilience have cemented its status as a macro asset and financial reserve in the crypto ecosystem.

Ethereum's Uses:

Ethereum powers a broad array of decentralized applications. It's the foundation of DeFi platforms (like Uniswap and Aave), NFT marketplaces, and DAOs that govern projects via token-based voting. Beyond finance and art, Ethereum supports blockchain gaming, social platforms, and supply chain tracking. It also hosts most stablecoins (like USDC), facilitating global, near-instant digital dollar transfers. In the future, institutions plan to use Ethereum to tokenize real-world assets like bonds and funds, reinforcing its role as a general-purpose platform for programmable value and ownership.

Community and Developer Ecosystem: Bitcoin vs Ethereum

Bitcoin and Ethereum have vibrant, open-source ecosystems fueled by developers, entrepreneurs, investors, and enthusiasts, though each reflects its culture and priorities.

Bitcoin's Community & Ecosystem:

Bitcoin's community is pragmatic and security-focused. Development through Bitcoin Core is slow and deliberate, with changes like SegWit, Taproot, and Lightning focused on scalability and privacy without compromising the base layer. A network of exchanges, miners, custodians, and nonprofits (like Brink) supports its infrastructure. The Lightning Network, in particular, has attracted a subcommunity building wallets, payments, and even smart contract-like functions. Sidechains like Liquid and RSK show that while conservative, Bitcoin's developers still explore expanded use cases in ways that preserve Bitcoin's core principles of decentralization and resilience.

Ethereum's Community & Ecosystem:

Ethereum's community is innovation-driven and collaborative. The Ethereum Foundation and EIPs steer development, but independent projects, hackathons, and DAOs power the broader ecosystem. Its vibrant application layer spans DeFi, NFTs, gaming, identity, and social networks. Layer-2 teams like Arbitrum, Optimism, and Polygon are integral to scaling efforts, and enterprise alliances (e.g., the EEA) show growing corporate interest. Ethereum embraces experimentation, which drives rapid evolution, sometimes with challenges like congestion or high fees, but also continuous progress.

Collaboration and Bridging Traditional Finance:

Both networks engage with traditional finance. Bitcoin's partnerships (e.g., Lightning integrations with fintech apps) are making BTC usable in real-world payments. Ethereum sees institutions using its network or stablecoins for settlements and asset tokenization. These collaborations reflect growing adoption and ecosystem maturity.

Bitcoin's community is laser-focused on secure, decentralized money, while Ethereum's community pushes the boundaries of decentralized applications. Together, they represent complementary pillars of blockchain's future—deeply supported by passionate, global communities.

Investment Perspectives: Bitcoin and Ethereum

From an investment standpoint, Bitcoin and Ethereum offer distinct but complementary value propositions that both institutional and retail investors increasingly recognize.

Bitcoin is widely viewed as a potential store of value, prized for its scarcity, resilience, and independence from centralized monetary policy. Its appeal as a hedge against inflation and macro uncertainty has grown, especially post-2024 halving and with the rise of regulated Bitcoin ETPs. Bitcoin's relatively low correlation to traditional assets adds portfolio diversification benefits, though its volatility demands prudent risk management. Today, a range of institutions, hedge funds, pensions, and retail platforms have integrated Bitcoin into portfolios, further legitimizing it as a long-term, accessible investment.

Ethereum is seen as a bet on the decentralized internet and financial innovation. Its value stems from being the core infrastructure for DeFi, NFTs, and tokenized assets. With Proof of Stake, ETH holders can now earn staking rewards (4–6% annually), adding an income dimension. Fee-burning (via EIP-1559) introduces deflationary potential, enhancing ETH's appeal as both a utility asset and a potential store of value. Institutional interest is growing, evidenced by Ether ETPs, though Ethereum carries more complexity and execution risk than Bitcoin.

Bitcoin vs Ethereum: Long-Term Outlooks

Predictions for Bitcoin and Ethereum over the next 5–10 years are generally optimistic, though they acknowledge risks and volatility. Both have defied skeptics, and their futures will be shaped by tech upgrades, regulatory developments, and global macro trends.

Bitcoin:

Bitcoin's long-term outlook is relatively straightforward. Post-2024 halving, its reduced issuance, and strong stock-to-flow dynamics appeal to long-term holders. Institutional adoption via ETPs and treasury allocations supports its narrative as digital sound money. With a slow-moving, conservative development path, Bitcoin avoids the execution risks that Ethereum faces. Its continued success largely depends on increased adoption as a hedge or reserve asset.

Ethereum:

Ethereum's future is more complex. It leads in decentralized applications but must execute a challenging roadmap (e.g., rollups, sharding) to sustain growth. While upgrades like the Merge and Shanghai laid the groundwork, the transition to a rollup-centric model raises concerns, particularly around fee capture and Layer-1 value retention. Some worry that Ethereum's base layer risks becoming a "commodity backbone" as more activity shifts to Layer-2s.

Competition and Fragmentation:

Ethereum also faces rising competition from newer Layer-1s, which offer faster throughput and different trade-offs. These rivals have chipped away at Ethereum's dominance, sparking debates on economic realignment (e.g., L2 revenue sharing). At the same time, Ethereum continues to evolve: the Pectra upgrade and falling fees in early 2025 signal ongoing innovation.

Bitcoin's simplicity and scarcity give it a steady, low-risk profile. Ethereum offers broader potential but carries greater uncertainty. Its success now hinges on delivering upgrades while preserving ecosystem cohesion and value capture, challenges that define its near-term narrative.
