Conclusion: Navigating the Bitcoin and Ethereum Landscape

Bitcoin and Ethereum each offer distinct yet complementary roles in shaping digital finance's future. Bitcoin stands as a secure, decentralized potential store of value, while Ethereum powers a dynamic ecosystem of decentralized applications and digital ownership. As of May 2025, both have matured significantly - Bitcoin with growing institutional adoption and Ethereum through major protocol upgrades and expanding use cases. BTC and ETH present unique opportunities for investors. Rather than an either/or decision, many portfolios include both to balance risk and utility. Despite volatility and evolving regulations, staying informed, patient, and engaged with the space has historically benefited long-term believers. Together, Bitcoin and Ethereum continue to redefine global finance and technology, cementing their roles as foundational pillars of the blockchain era.

Important Disclosures

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency, without a central bank or single administrator, that can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without the need for intermediaries.

Ethereum (ETH) is a decentralized, open–source blockchain with smart contract functionality. Ether is the native cryptocurrency of the platform. Amongst cryptocurrencies, Ether is second only to Bitcoin in market capitalization.

data.title: Nvidia's Growth Phase Is Still In Full Bloom
data.description:
Since my Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) Q1 preview, the stock has gained 2.5% in price following strong sentiment surrounding the Q1 release.

Nvidia had a remarkable Q1 FY2026, confirming its tremendous growth path continues at full speed while positioned as the greatest company in its sector. Quarterly revenue increased by 69% year-over-year to $44.1 billion, vastly outperforming the wider semiconductor ecosystem. The Data Center segment was incredibly strong, delivering $39.1 billion, a 73% year-over-year growth, as commercial enterprises and cloud providers adopted Nvidia's AI infrastructure overwhelmingly. This market is truly a world built for two, with Nvidia and AI hyperscalers in perfect harmony. Net income increased significantly, and although a one-time charge for inventory ($4.5 billion) hampered reported margins, adjusted earnings per share ("EPS") reached $0.81 ($0.96 without the charge).

Nvidia also further cemented its strategic positioning by announcing Stargate UAE, a historic collaboration with G42, OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL), SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and Cisco (CSCO), to create a 1-gigawatt ("GW") AI supercomputing cluster in Abu Dhabi. This initiative is born to die without enduring consensus effort and geopolitical cooperation, but it's crucial for the soaring worldwide demand for AI and puts Nvidia in the limelight of current international relations. There could be no clearer evidence than the UAE deal of Nvidia's expanding centrality in the global AI economy. Indeed, it’s evident: Nvidia will continue to move forward on its destined path of prosperity, against all odds.

Nvidia Is Defying The Odds

Nvidia delivered outstanding results for Q1 FY2026, showcasing resilience and devotion to its customers and industry. It posted remarkable revenue of $44.1 billion, up 69% year-over-year, primarily due to surging demand for AI solutions. The data center segment achieved $39.1 billion in revenue, representing approximately 89% of total sales, reflecting heavy adoption of Nvidia's modernized HGX systems (Nvidia's flagship AI server platform) for generative AI applications. Gaming rebounded very well, generating $3.8 billion in revenue (up 42% year-over-year) from the introduction of the new RTX 50-series GPUs and AI-generated DLSS 4. Professional Visualization grew modestly to $509 million, an improvement of 19% year-over-year, while Automotive and Robotics realized a substantial increase of 72% year-over-year with revenue of $567 million due to adoption of DRIVE and Jetson data collection methods.

Needless to say, the 2022 low in Nvidia's stock price is now clearly behind us, and management is riding a historical wave of unprecedented growth that founder Huang had believed in for many years. To me, Huang represents a savage devotion to his craft, industry, team, and responsibilities. He is primarily why I am so heavily invested in Nvidia, holding the stock at 15% of my total portfolio.

Margins were pressured temporarily due to a $4.5 billion inventory charge related to new U.S.–China export regulations, reducing the Generally Accepted Accounting Principles ("GAAP") gross margin to 60.5% (down from 78%). The adjusted gross margin remained solid at 71.3%, signaling Nvidia has strong pricing power. Operating expenses rose 44% year-over-year to $5 billion as Nvidia continued to ramp research and development ("R&D") levels aggressively, but operating leverage improved significantly to offset some of those additional costs due to surging revenues. Management’s resilience amid external pressures is particularly noteworthy, especially considering just how tough the geopolitical environment is. Indeed, Nvidia stock has almost become a religion to its investors amid Huang’s tenacious leadership and resolve.

Earnings remained solid; GAAP EPS was $0.76 and adjusted EPS was $0.81 ($0.96 when adjusted for the China export charge). CFO Colette Kress stressed record demand for Nvidia’s computing platforms and guided $45 billion in revenue for Q2 2026 (up 67% year-over-year). Jensen Huang was succinct in communicating Nvidia’s strategic differentiator in his statement: "AI is becoming essential infrastructure, and Nvidia is at the forefront of enabling this transformation."

Cash generation was similarly impressive, with operating cash flow in Q1 of $27.4 billion—almost double that of the previous year. Nvidia also aggressively returned cash to shareholders, with $14.1 billion in stock repurchases (nearly twice last year’s pace). The dividend is kept small, which indicates a clear preference to invest in the business by buying back shares. I like a business that takes deep care of its shareholders like a family, and Nvidia fits the bill perfectly. As AI is the anthem of success in the modern world, Nvidia’s shareholders are both well taken care of and positioned to ride much further growth.

Nvidia Has Developed Into A Rare Generational Stock

Nvidia's leadership position in the AI ecosystem remains unique, supported by hardware innovation, and its deeply integrated software ecosystem. The launch of Nvidia's next-gen Blackwell NVL72 AI supercomputer and the commitment to mass manufacture by large OEMs and cloud operators demonstrates a very strong demand for Nvidia's advanced hardware. The Blackwell architecture is the successor to Hopper, and is also better suited for AI training and inference, meaning that Nvidia will maintain its hardware leadership position indefinitely. Furthermore, the ramp up in adoption of Nvidia's Grace CPU and Grace-Hopper superchips is also growing as they further penetrate the data center market, with the recent announcement of the Grace Blackwell DGX Station also showing that Nvidia’s hardware portfolio is wholly integrated. These are market-leading products that cannot be disputed.

A bit of history: Nvidia has morphed from an unknown chip company decades ago to the undisputed champion of semiconductors today. It is a story—still in process—of ingenious business greatness. Huang has had many setbacks in his years. Particularly early on with the NV1 failure in 1995, when the company’s first chip was a commercial failure. Rather than folding, Huang scrapped NV1 and went big on RIVA 128 in 1997. This was a breakout success. I highlight this because Nvidia investors should know—this is a remarkable company founded by a remarkable man. When you invest in Nvidia, you take a grounded bet on a relentless entrepreneur who has both tasted failure and channeled that into present money, power, and glory.

Beyond hardware, Nvidia's software ecosystem, centered around CUDA, remains its greatest competitive moat, with more than 4 million developers globally. There are multiple open-source frameworks including NeMo, TensorRT, cuDNN, Triton, and Isaac SDK that simplify and enhance the deployment of AI on Nvidia's hardware. This helps to lock in customers with switching costs aided by long-term scalability. The same integration of hardware and software significantly reduces barriers to enterprise-level adoption and cybersecurity.

Lastly, Nvidia's involvement in the Stargate UAE initiative with G42, OpenAI, Oracle, SoftBank, and Cisco further showcases its proactive attitude to mitigating geopolitical risks beyond the traditional U.S. and China landscape. A new, sizable 1GW AI data center will be operational in Abu Dhabi to serve as a global hub for AI computing. Nvidia will maintain its strategic importance by being central to projects such as Stargate UAE. In other words, Nvidia is the primary architect of the world's evolving AI infrastructure.

In a time of heightened geopolitical risk right now, it is not only important, but necessary, for the right long-term U.S. alliances (like with the UAE) to be made. Through such alliances, history's lessons can be endured, and the future forged for market protection from any further peril. At the heart of this are the U.S. energy supply constraints, which require the UAE and other allied countries to be energy partners to America. This is especially important to support U.S. AI and robotics supremacy in an aggressive effort to create enough gross domestic product ("GDP") growth to counter interest payments and pay down federal debt for healthier macroeconomics and geopolitics globally. China is off the table as a reliable energy partner—for now. Whichever alliances are formed by the U.S., one element is for certain: Nvidia will be front and center.

The Robotics Opportunity Is The Greatest Of All Time

Robotics is a significant opportunity for growth, and Nvidia sees an opportunity to strengthen its strategic advantage at the point of intelligent automation across factories, logistics, and autonomous systems. The robotics product suite that Nvidia offers includes advanced hardware, world-class simulation tools, and integrated AI-based software that differentiates it from the competition in the rapidly growing robotics sector. As the road gets tough for Nvidia as initial AI hyperscaler capex budget growth begins to slow (or potentially even contract), I find it likely that robotics at scale will be a strong way for Nvidia to continue compounding returns, potentially avoiding cyclicality in its stock price in the medium to long term. In short, many analysts are underestimating the AI opportunity, and we must remember that Nvidia is the heart of this movement.

At the center of Nvidia's strategy in robotics is the Jetson platform, which offers AI modules that are compact and powerful for use in robots, drones, and edge computing. The Jetson module integrates with Isaac SDK and Isaac Sim, allowing developers to prototype, train, and deploy advanced robotic solutions quickly. Further, the Omniverse platform integrates simulation with 3D accuracy and digital twin functionality. These platforms and technologies lower product development time. For example, BMW (OTCPK:BAMXF, OTC:BMWKY) has successfully leveraged Omniverse with Isaac Sim and enhanced manufacturing efficiencies by 30%.

Nvidia has gained traction with leading companies in different industries, including BMW, Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY, OTCPK:SMAWF, OTCPK:SMEGF), Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF, OTCPK:SBGSY), and General Motors (GM), who are beginning to use Nvidia's robotics platform for AI-driven automation. Users achieve practical value such as reduced downtime and increased productivity that comes with robotic operations. Even new entrants like Figure are leveraging Nvidia's ecosystem to rapidly innovate in advanced humanoid robotics.

With robot sales growth projected to exceed $215 billion by 2030, Nvidia is well-positioned with strategic advantage. As Elon Musk (a profound inspiration of mine) has stated, robotics could be the greatest opportunity of all time. I believe so, too, and I’m positioning myself to invest aggressively in it.

Nvidia’s strengths not only lie in its deployment capacity but in its unique capability to simulate and prototype—in other words, to take products from virtual deployment to real-world use in a rapid fashion. I believe we are going to see new capex demands from Big Tech companies seeking robotics-related semiconductors, but also new Big Robotics companies that will emerge that will be buying Nvidia’s products hand over fist to fuel their intelligent technologies.

Edge AI (processing locally on hardware devices) will also be critical to robotics collaboration. The company recently announced advanced humanoid robot reference designs (e.g., Isaac GR00T), which highlight Nvidia's assertive approach to lead a future in the robotics industry. Further, given Nvidia's experience with establishing new technology markets, its increasing robotics position represents a real long-term growth opportunity. Nvidia is already off to the races with robotics—again, Huang is a clever man.

I Believe Nvidia Can Overcome All Risks

While Nvidia checks many of the key boxes of an attractive growth company, investors must pay attention to several key risk factors that could limit momentum. That said, even with roadblocks, this is the kind of company that will persevere, overcome them, and win out in the end. Nvidia is committed to being the dominant leader of the AI and robotics opportunity at the advanced semiconductor level through thick and thin.

Geopolitical and regulatory tensions, primarily around the U.S.-China trade issues, are prime threats. The recent U.S. policies restricting sales of advanced AI chips from Nvidia (e.g., the H20) resulted in a $4.5 billion write-down of inventory. Further, Huang indicated those policies that were intended to restrict Chinese competition risk backfiring, inadvertently assisting the competition which could affect the U.S. technological lead. Right now, it appears that these restrictions are going to ease, opening a critical door for better U.S.-China cooperation. However, if tensions escalate, fluctuations and volatility may remain and China may push much more aggressively to develop its own alternatives to Nvidia to compete in the ultra-advanced semiconductor market. I'm certain that Nvidia will leverage its geopolitical connections to support fair and judicious global outcomes between the West and East, while supporting the U.S.-led world order primarily.

I’ve already mentioned the AI hyperscaler capex threat earlier in this piece, but it is worth mentioning. Semiconductors are traditionally a cyclical business, but this era defined by AI has a substantial likelihood of being different. Nonetheless, no matter the course, through time I have no doubt that Nvidia will continue to thrive through any peaks and valleys because of its exceptional, rare, and full-of-class management.

Competition will remain a strategic aspect to monitor. Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) forthcoming MI300 accelerators and Intel's (INTC) Habana-based Gaudi chips, in addition to their efforts to build open software ecosystems, pose a challenge to Nvidia's unquestioned leadership in the AI accelerator market. That said, a world where Nvidia is not the de facto leader is almost irrational to consider at this point. Its primary moat is essentially impenetrable due to its lead and infrastructure. This moat extends beyond pure operations down to the core of Huang and management's values, philosophy, and ethics structure.

That said, established and emerging competitors with a sustainable process of continuous innovation can chip away at, or share in, Nvidia's lead. The risk of disruptive technologies (like quantum computing) does exacerbate the situation. Yet, I see this more like other AI competitors riding on Nvidia’s coattails or trying for second best rather than genuinely having a shot at being the one and only. There can only be one that’s defined as the greatest, and in AI, it is Nvidia.

Value Investors See Opportunity In Fear

Although the market is well aware of Nvidia’s incredible industry positioning now, it wasn’t always this way. But it’s still not too late to invest in Nvidia—the AI and robotics era is just getting started.

My current best-case price target for the stock is $190 in 12 months. I believe there will be macro tailwinds in 2026, especially from deregulation and pro-growth tax initiatives, details of international trade agreements between the U.S. and allies supporting purchase sentiment, and a further easing of U.S.-China restrictions. Revenue only has to hit about $230 billion by the middle of calendar 2026 for this to be possible. Even if only a $180 stock price is achieved, that will still be a great return from the present $140 of 28.5% in just a year, which is essentially the "market-best" 30% annual return I look for.

My Nvidia investment is already up by 17% since I bought it across the last six months or so. I bought when the market was afraid due to Trump tariffs and China AI initiatives leading to a low Nvidia stock price, because I saw all the macro signals that a recovery was not only imminent, but also that new all-time highs were just around the corner.

$230 billion in revenue with a net margin of 53.5% means a net income of $123.05 billion. If total shares are reduced through buybacks by the careful and thoughtful management team, we’re looking at a net income division by about 24.55 billion shares in a conservative outcome. The diluted weighted outstanding share count is currently 24.734 billion. $123.05 billion divided by 24.55 billion is $5 per share when rounded—this is my trailing 12-month ("TTM") diluted EPS estimate for May 2026.

However, to get to the final normalized EPS estimate, we have to account for stock-based compensation, impairments or write-downs, one-time litigation settlements, restructuring charges, tax adjustments, and gains or losses from asset sales that may occur. As a result, my TTM normalized EPS estimate for Nvidia in May 2026 is $4.75.

Nvidia’s current TTM P/E non-GAAP ratio is 43.5. We should almost certainly expect some multiple compression over the next 12 months as even with positive macro factors Nvidia is facing natural decelerating growth amid its peak ascent in the AI industry. To be conservative while remaining optimistic, I forecast a TTM P/E non-GAAP multiple of 37.5 in 12 months’ time. That places my official price target at $178.125. For simplicity—$180. That’s a 28.5% return in just 12 months. Nvidia is a Strong Buy, and I’m sticking to my guns on the stock.

Nvidia Is The Best In The World

Nvidia is a truly remarkable company with a truly remarkable founder and CEO, the all-time greatest semiconductor pioneer, Jensen Huang. The company’s enormous opportunity for growth is not over, with strong demand in AI data centers and exposure to robotics at scale in the medium to long term. The market was right to go wild for Nvidia; there is no AI bubble, and robotics is the opportunity that is Nvidia’s next great moment to shine once this peak culminates. If you bet on anything, bet on Jensen Huang’s tenacious attitude to semiconductor design dominance, his devotion to the AI field, and his ability to be agile in the face of all challenges, macro changes, and threats. Huang is a real leader—he is one who should be respected, even revered.

As such, I hold my Nvidia stock with confidence, knowing its returns are in the hands of a man with the vision to steward my capital through any hardships that may arise. I am a devout Nvidia bull, and I believe you should be too.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
